name: SIGHUP
description: Hangup — sent when the controlling terminal is closed, or to request config reload from daemons.

name: SIGINT
description: Interrupt — sent by the terminal when Ctrl+C is pressed. Requests graceful termination.

name: SIGQUIT
description: Quit — sent by Ctrl+\. Like SIGINT but produces a core dump for debugging.

name: SIGILL
description: Illegal instruction — sent when the CPU encounters an unrecognised or privileged instruction.

name: SIGTRAP
description: Trace/breakpoint trap — used by debuggers to implement breakpoints and single-stepping.

name: SIGABRT
description: Abort — sent by abort(3) when the program calls it directly to indicate a fatal error.

name: SIGBUS
description: Bus error — indicates a memory access alignment fault or access to an unmapped address.

name: SIGFPE
description: Floating-point exception — sent on arithmetic errors such as division by zero or overflow.

name: SIGKILL
description: Kill — immediately terminates the process. Cannot be caught, blocked, or ignored. Use as last resort.

name: SIGUSR1
description: User-defined signal 1 — application-specific. Commonly used to trigger log rotation or reload.

name: SIGSEGV
description: Segmentation violation — sent when a process makes an invalid memory reference (null pointer, buffer overflow, etc.).

name: SIGUSR2
description: User-defined signal 2 — application-specific. Meaning is defined entirely by the application.

name: SIGPIPE
description: Broken pipe — sent when writing to a pipe with no readers. Commonly suppressed in network servers.

name: SIGALRM
description: Alarm clock — sent when a timer set by alarm(2) or setitimer(2) expires.

name: SIGTERM
description: Termination — the default signal sent by kill. Requests graceful shutdown; can be caught to perform cleanup.

name: SIGSTKFLT
description: Stack fault on coprocessor (Linux-specific). Rarely used in modern systems.

name: SIGCHLD
description: Child stopped or terminated — sent to a parent process when a child process stops or terminates. Used with wait(2).

name: SIGCONT
description: Continue — resumes a process that was stopped by SIGSTOP or SIGTSTP. Cannot be blocked.

name: SIGSTOP
description: Stop — pauses a process. Cannot be caught, blocked, or ignored. Resume with SIGCONT.

name: SIGTSTP
description: Terminal stop — sent by Ctrl+Z. Like SIGSTOP but can be caught (e.g., to clean up terminal state before stopping).

name: SIGTTIN
description: Background read from terminal — sent to a background process that tries to read from the terminal.

name: SIGTTOU
description: Background write to terminal — sent to a background process that tries to write to the terminal.

name: SIGURG
description: Urgent condition on socket — sent when out-of-band (urgent) data arrives on a socket.

name: SIGXCPU
description: CPU time limit exceeded — sent when a process exceeds its CPU time resource limit (setrlimit).

name: SIGXFSZ
description: File size limit exceeded — sent when a process tries to create a file larger than the RLIMIT_FSIZE limit.

name: SIGVTALRM
description: Virtual timer alarm — sent when a virtual (user-mode only) timer set by setitimer expires.

name: SIGPROF
description: Profiling timer alarm — sent when a profiling timer (user+system time) expires. Used by profilers.

name: SIGWINCH
description: Window resize — sent when the terminal window size changes. Used by interactive programs like vim and htop.

name: SIGIO
description: I/O possible — sent when I/O becomes possible on a file descriptor marked with O_ASYNC.

name: SIGPWR
description: Power failure — sent by init when a UPS signals imminent power loss.

name: SIGSYS
description: Bad system call — sent when a process makes a system call with invalid arguments or a seccomp filter rejects it.
